method: Pre-training a smaller general-purpose language representation model called DistilBERT.
Leveraging knowledge distillation during the pre-training phase to reduce model size.
Introducing a triple loss function combining language modeling (Lmlm), distillation (Lce), and cosine-distance (Lcos) losses to align student and teacher hidden states.
Student (DistilBERT) architecture maintains the general structure of BERT, but token-type embeddings and the pooler are removed, and the number of layers is reduced by a factor of 2.
Initializing the student model from the teacher by taking one layer out of two.
Using a softmax-temperature for distillation loss calculation, applied to both student and teacher at training time, and set to 1 at inference.
Demonstrating capabilities for on-device computations through proof-of-concept and comparative studies.
datasets: Evaluated on the GLUE benchmark tasks (CoLA, MNLI, MRPC, QNLI, QQP, RTE, SST-2, STS-B, WNLI) for language understanding capabilities.
Evaluated on IMDb for test accuracy.
Evaluated on SQuAD 1.1 for EM/F1 on the development set.
limitations: DistilBERT retains 97% of BERT's language understanding capabilities, indicating a minor performance reduction (3%) compared to the larger teacher model.
Performance on various downstream tasks (GLUE, IMDb, SQuAD 1.1) is generally comparable to but slightly lower than BERT-base.
